primitives.name: Category
primitives.formalStatement: A category C consists of a collection of objects ob(C), a collection of morphisms hom(C) between objects, an identity morphism id_A for each object A, and a composition operation that is associative and respects identities.

dependencies.description: Categories generalize the structure of sets and functions
Proofs about categories use logical reasoning

compositionRules.yields: Group as a single-object category where all morphisms are invertible
compositionRules.example: A group G viewed as a category with one object * and morphisms Hom(*,*) = G

applicabilityPatterns: Analyzing structure-preserving maps between mathematical objects
Identifying universal properties in algebraic structures
Abstracting common patterns across different areas of mathematics

primitives.name: Functor
primitives.formalStatement: A functor F: C -> D maps objects of C to objects of D and morphisms of C to morphisms of D, preserving identity morphisms F(id_A) = id_{F(A)} and composition F(g . f) = F(g) . F(f).

dependencies.description: Functors are maps between categories
Functors generalize group homomorphisms to arbitrary categories
Functors generalize linear maps between vector spaces

compositionRules.yields: Composite functor G . F: C -> E
conditions: Codomain of F equals domain of G
compositionRules.example: Forgetful functor from groups to sets, composed with free functor from sets to groups, gives the free-forgetful adjunction

applicabilityPatterns: Translating problems between different mathematical frameworks
Identifying when a map preserves essential structure
Building bridges between algebraic and geometric viewpoints

primitives.name: Natural Transformation
primitives.formalStatement: A natural transformation eta: F => G between functors F, G: C -> D is a family of morphisms eta_A: F(A) -> G(A) for each object A in C, such that for every morphism f: A -> B in C, the diagram commutes: G(f) . eta_A = eta_B . F(f).

dependencies.description: Natural transformations are morphisms between functors
Naturality is defined within category theory

compositionRules.yields: Whiskered natural transformation (horizontal composition)
conditions: Functor domain/codomain compatible with transformation components
compositionRules.example: Composing a natural transformation with a functor to shift the naturality square across categories

applicabilityPatterns: Comparing two different ways to transform mathematical structures
Establishing canonical relationships between functors
Verifying that a family of maps is independent of arbitrary choices

primitives.name: Yoneda Lemma
primitives.formalStatement: For any functor F: C^op -> Set and object A in C, there is a natural bijection Nat(Hom(-, A), F) ≅ F(A), given by evaluating at the identity morphism id_A.
primitives.computationalForm: Given a natural transformation alpha: Hom(-, A) => F, compute alpha_A(id_A) to obtain the corresponding element of F(A). Conversely, given x in F(A), define alpha_B(f) = F(f)(x) for each f: B -> A.

dependencies.description: Yoneda classifies natural transformations
Yoneda relates functors to representable presheaves
Stated within the framework of category theory

compositionRules.yields: Embedding of any category into its presheaf category (Yoneda embedding)
compositionRules.example: Every group G embeds fully faithfully into the functor category [G^op, Set] via the Yoneda embedding

applicabilityPatterns: Determining when an object is uniquely characterized by its relationships
Reducing questions about natural transformations to concrete element computations
Proving representability of functors

primitives.name: Adjunction
primitives.formalStatement: An adjunction between categories C and D is a pair of functors F: C -> D (left adjoint) and G: D -> C (right adjoint) with a natural bijection Hom_D(F(A), B) ≅ Hom_C(A, G(B)) for all objects A in C, B in D.

dependencies.description: Adjunctions relate pairs of functors
Unit and counit are natural transformations
Adjunctions are defined between categories

compositionRules.yields: Free-forgetful adjunction between groups and sets
conditions: Forgetful functor has a left adjoint
compositionRules.example: The free group functor F: Set -> Grp is left adjoint to the forgetful functor U: Grp -> Set

applicabilityPatterns: Finding the best approximation of one type of structure by another
Establishing free-forgetful relationships between algebraic structures
Optimizing constructions with universal properties

primitives.name: Monad
primitives.formalStatement: A monad on a category C is an endofunctor T: C -> C equipped with natural transformations eta: Id => T (unit) and mu: T^2 => T (multiplication) satisfying associativity mu . T(mu) = mu . mu_T and unit laws mu . T(eta) = mu . eta_T = id_T.

dependencies.description: Every adjunction gives rise to a monad
A monad is an endofunctor with extra structure
Unit and multiplication are natural transformations

compositionRules.yields: Kleisli category for sequential composition of effectful computations
compositionRules.example: The list monad captures nondeterminism: T(X) = List(X), unit wraps in singleton, multiplication flattens nested lists

applicabilityPatterns: Sequencing computations with side effects in functional programming
Encoding algebraic theories as categorical structures
Chaining transformations that produce structured output

primitives.name: Universal Property
primitives.formalStatement: An object U in a category C has a universal property with respect to a functor F if for every object X and morphism f: F(X) -> Y (or f: Y -> F(X)), there exists a unique morphism u: X -> U (or u: U -> X) making the appropriate diagram commute.

dependencies.description: Universal properties are stated within categories
Universal properties often involve functors

compositionRules.yields: Characterization of free groups via universal property
conditions: Construction satisfies the universal mapping property
compositionRules.example: The free group on a set S is the unique group (up to isomorphism) through which every set map from S to any group G factors

applicabilityPatterns: Defining mathematical constructions by what they do rather than how they are built
Proving uniqueness of constructions up to isomorphism
Identifying when an object is the best solution to a mapping problem

primitives.name: Limits and Colimits
primitives.formalStatement: A limit of a diagram D: J -> C is an object lim D with projection morphisms pi_j: lim D -> D(j) that is universal among all cones over D. A colimit is the dual: an object colim D with injection morphisms that is universal among all cocones.

dependencies.description: Limits are defined by their universal property
Limits exist within categories

compositionRules.yields: Products, coproducts, equalizers, coequalizers in Set
compositionRules.example: The product of sets A and B is the Cartesian product A x B with projection maps, which is a limit of the discrete two-object diagram

applicabilityPatterns: Constructing products, intersections, and pullbacks in abstract settings
Building new objects from diagrams of existing objects
Ensuring completeness of categorical constructions

primitives.name: Shannon Entropy
primitives.formalStatement: For a discrete random variable X with probability mass function P(x), the Shannon entropy is H(X) = -sum_x P(x) log_2 P(x), measuring the average information content in bits per symbol.
primitives.computationalForm: For each outcome x with probability p(x) > 0, compute -p(x) * log2(p(x)). Sum over all outcomes. Convention: 0 * log(0) = 0.

dependencies.description: Entropy is defined over probability distributions
Continuous entropy uses integration: h(X) = -integral f(x) log f(x) dx
Entropy values are real numbers

compositionRules.yields: Entropy of joint distributions and conditional entropy
compositionRules.example: H(X,Y) = H(X) + H(Y|X), the chain rule of entropy

applicabilityPatterns: Measuring uncertainty or surprise in a random source
Determining minimum bits needed to encode a message
Quantifying information content of a probability distribution

primitives.name: Mutual Information
primitives.formalStatement: The mutual information between random variables X and Y is I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y) = sum_{x,y} P(x,y) log(P(x,y) / (P(x)P(y))), measuring the information shared between X and Y.
primitives.computationalForm: Compute joint entropy H(X,Y), marginal entropies H(X) and H(Y), then I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y). Alternatively, compute KL divergence D(P(X,Y) || P(X)P(Y)).

dependencies.description: Mutual information is defined via entropy
Requires joint and marginal distributions

compositionRules.yields: Data processing inequality: I(X;Z) <= I(X;Y) when X->Y->Z form a Markov chain
compositionRules.example: Processing data through a noisy channel can only lose information, never gain it

applicabilityPatterns: Measuring how much knowing one variable tells about another
Feature selection in machine learning by maximizing relevance
Quantifying statistical dependence between variables

primitives.name: Channel Capacity
primitives.formalStatement: The capacity of a discrete memoryless channel is C = max_{P(x)} I(X;Y), the maximum mutual information over all input distributions. Shannon's channel coding theorem states that reliable communication is possible at any rate R < C and impossible for R > C.
primitives.computationalForm: Compute mutual information I(X;Y) for each valid input distribution P(x). Use iterative algorithms like Blahut-Arimoto to find the maximizing distribution. C = max I(X;Y).

dependencies.description: Channel capacity maximizes mutual information
Entropy bounds the capacity
Capacity is an optimization problem over input distributions

compositionRules.yields: Rate-distortion theory for lossy compression
compositionRules.example: For a binary symmetric source with crossover probability p, channel capacity C = 1 - H(p)

applicabilityPatterns: Determining the maximum reliable data rate for a communication channel
Designing error-correcting codes that approach capacity
Bounding achievable compression ratios

primitives.name: Kullback-Leibler Divergence
primitives.formalStatement: The Kullback-Leibler divergence from distribution Q to distribution P is D_KL(P || Q) = sum_x P(x) log(P(x)/Q(x)), measuring how P diverges from Q. It is non-negative (Gibbs' inequality) and zero iff P = Q.
primitives.computationalForm: For each x where P(x) > 0, compute P(x) * log(P(x)/Q(x)). Sum over all x. If Q(x) = 0 where P(x) > 0, D_KL = infinity.

dependencies.description: KL divergence decomposes into cross-entropy minus entropy
Defined over probability distributions

compositionRules.yields: I(X;Y) = D_KL(P(X,Y) || P(X)P(Y)), mutual information as KL divergence from independence
compositionRules.example: Mutual information measures divergence of the joint distribution from the product of marginals

applicabilityPatterns: Measuring the difference between two probability distributions
Defining loss functions for variational inference and generative models
Quantifying information loss when approximating one distribution by another

primitives.name: Huffman Coding
primitives.formalStatement: Huffman coding is an optimal prefix-free coding scheme that assigns shorter codewords to more probable symbols, achieving average code length within 1 bit of the entropy H(X).
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Create a leaf node for each symbol with its probability. 2. Repeatedly merge the two lowest-probability nodes into a new parent. 3. Assign 0/1 to left/right branches. 4. Read codeword for each symbol from root to leaf.

dependencies.description: Huffman coding approaches the entropy lower bound
Requires symbol probabilities

compositionRules.yields: Optimal lossless data compression with provable bounds
compositionRules.example: For English text with known letter frequencies, Huffman coding achieves ~4.7 bits/character vs 8 bits for ASCII

applicabilityPatterns: Compressing data when symbol frequencies are known
Building optimal variable-length codes for lossless compression
Encoding symbols with minimum average code length

primitives.name: Source Coding Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: For a discrete memoryless source with entropy H(X), the minimum achievable average code length per symbol with lossless compression satisfies L >= H(X), and there exist codes achieving L < H(X) + 1 bit.
primitives.computationalForm: Compute the entropy H(X) of the source. This gives the fundamental lower bound on compression. Construct a Huffman code or arithmetic code to approach this bound. For block coding of length n, L_n/n -> H(X) as n -> infinity.

dependencies.description: The entropy is the fundamental limit of lossless compression
Huffman coding achieves near-optimal compression

compositionRules.yields: Joint source-channel coding theorem (separation theorem)
compositionRules.example: Shannon showed that source coding and channel coding can be optimized separately without loss of optimality

applicabilityPatterns: Determining the fundamental limits of data compression
Proving optimality of coding schemes
Understanding the relationship between information content and code length

primitives.name: Error Correction Codes
primitives.formalStatement: An (n, k, d) error-correcting code encodes k information symbols into n code symbols with minimum Hamming distance d, capable of detecting d-1 errors and correcting floor((d-1)/2) errors.
primitives.computationalForm: Encode: multiply message vector m (length k) by generator matrix G to produce codeword c = mG (length n). Decode: compute syndrome s = rH^T for received vector r. If s = 0, no error. Otherwise, use syndrome to identify and correct error pattern.

dependencies.description: Error correction codes approach channel capacity
Linear codes use matrix multiplication over finite fields
Codes are defined over finite fields

compositionRules.yields: Capacity-approaching code design (turbo codes, LDPC)
compositionRules.example: LDPC codes can achieve rates within 0.0045 dB of the Shannon limit on binary symmetric channels

applicabilityPatterns: Protecting data from corruption during transmission or storage
Designing reliable communication systems
Adding controlled redundancy to detect and fix errors

primitives.name: Fourier Transform
primitives.formalStatement: The Fourier transform of an integrable function f: R -> C is F{f}(xi) = integral_{-inf}^{inf} f(t) e^{-2pi i xi t} dt, mapping the function from the time domain to the frequency domain.
primitives.computationalForm: For continuous signals, evaluate the integral numerically using quadrature. For discrete signals of length N, use the DFT: X[k] = sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{-2pi i kn/N}.

dependencies.description: Fourier transform generalizes Fourier series from periodic to aperiodic functions
The transform is defined as an integral
Uses complex exponentials and analytic properties

compositionRules.yields: Unified spectral analysis for periodic and aperiodic signals
compositionRules.example: A windowed periodic signal uses the Fourier transform, reducing to Fourier series coefficients as window length approaches the period

applicabilityPatterns: Analyzing frequency content of signals
Solving differential equations by transforming to the frequency domain
Converting between time-domain and frequency-domain representations

primitives.name: Fast Fourier Transform
primitives.formalStatement: The FFT computes the Discrete Fourier Transform of N points in O(N log N) operations by exploiting the periodicity and symmetry of the complex roots of unity, recursively decomposing the DFT into smaller sub-problems (Cooley-Tukey algorithm).
primitives.computationalForm: Split x[n] into even-indexed x_e[n] = x[2n] and odd-indexed x_o[n] = x[2n+1]. Compute N/2-point DFTs of each. Combine: X[k] = X_e[k] + W_N^k * X_o[k], X[k+N/2] = X_e[k] - W_N^k * X_o[k], where W_N = e^{-2pi i/N}. Requires N to be a power of 2 (or use mixed-radix variants).

dependencies.description: FFT is an efficient algorithm for computing the DFT
FFT exploits roots of unity on the unit circle

compositionRules.yields: O(N log N) convolution via spectral multiplication
compositionRules.example: Multiply two polynomials of degree n in O(n log n) by FFT, pointwise multiply, inverse FFT

applicabilityPatterns: Computing frequency spectra of large datasets efficiently
Enabling real-time audio and image processing
Accelerating convolution and correlation computations

primitives.name: Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: A bandlimited signal f(t) with maximum frequency B can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples taken at rate f_s >= 2B (the Nyquist rate): f(t) = sum_n f(n/f_s) sinc(f_s * t - n).
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Determine the bandwidth B of the signal. 2. Sample at rate f_s >= 2B. 3. Reconstruct using sinc interpolation: f(t) = sum_n x[n] * sinc((t - n/f_s) * f_s). In practice, use windowed sinc or polyphase filters.

dependencies.description: Sampling theorem is proved via Fourier analysis
Discrete sampling connects to Fourier series of periodic extensions

compositionRules.yields: Complete analog-to-digital and digital-to-analog conversion pipeline
compositionRules.example: Audio CD sampling at 44.1 kHz captures all frequencies up to 22.05 kHz, exceeding the 20 kHz human hearing limit

applicabilityPatterns: Determining the minimum sampling rate for faithful signal digitization
Understanding aliasing artifacts when undersampling
Designing analog-to-digital converters

primitives.name: Convolution Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: The Fourier transform converts convolution to pointwise multiplication: F{f * g} = F{f} . F{g}, where (f * g)(t) = integral f(tau) g(t - tau) d(tau). Equivalently, F{f . g} = F{f} * F{g}.
primitives.computationalForm: To convolve f and g: 1. Compute F{f} and F{g} using FFT. 2. Multiply pointwise: H = F{f} . F{g}. 3. Inverse FFT to get f * g = F^{-1}{H}. Total cost: O(N log N) instead of O(N^2).

dependencies.description: The theorem relates convolution and multiplication via the Fourier transform
Convolution is defined as an integral

compositionRules.yields: Fast convolution algorithm
compositionRules.example: Applying a FIR filter of length M to a signal of length N takes O((N+M) log(N+M)) instead of O(NM)

applicabilityPatterns: Speeding up convolution operations in signal processing
Understanding how filters operate in the frequency domain
Analyzing linear time-invariant systems

primitives.name: Digital Filter Design
primitives.formalStatement: A digital filter is a linear time-invariant system characterized by its impulse response h[n] or equivalently its transfer function H(z) = sum_n h[n] z^{-n}. FIR filters have finite impulse response; IIR filters have infinite impulse response with feedback.
primitives.computationalForm: FIR: y[n] = sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]. IIR: y[n] = sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k] - sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k]. Design via window method (FIR) or bilinear transform (IIR).

dependencies.description: Filter design uses frequency-domain specifications
Transfer functions are expressed in the z-domain

compositionRules.yields: Real-time spectral analysis with windowed FFT filtering
compositionRules.example: Low-pass Butterworth filter designed with bilinear transform, then applied in real-time to audio signals

applicabilityPatterns: Separating desired signal components from noise
Implementing low-pass, high-pass, and band-pass frequency selection
Designing audio effects and equalization systems

primitives.name: Z-Transform
primitives.formalStatement: The Z-transform of a discrete-time signal x[n] is X(z) = sum_{n=-inf}^{inf} x[n] z^{-n}, where z is a complex variable. The region of convergence (ROC) determines where the series converges.
primitives.computationalForm: For causal signals, evaluate the one-sided sum X(z) = sum_{n=0}^{inf} x[n] z^{-n}. For rational X(z) = B(z)/A(z), find poles and zeros. The ROC extends outward from the outermost pole for causal signals.

dependencies.description: Z-transform is the discrete-time analog of the Laplace transform; evaluating on the unit circle gives the DTFT
Z-transform analysis uses complex analysis (poles, zeros, ROC)

compositionRules.yields: Complete discrete-time system analysis via transfer functions
compositionRules.example: Z-transform converts a difference equation y[n] - 0.5y[n-1] = x[n] into algebraic form Y(z)(1 - 0.5z^{-1}) = X(z)

applicabilityPatterns: Analyzing discrete-time linear systems using algebra instead of difference equations
Determining stability from pole locations in the z-plane
Converting between time-domain and transform-domain representations of discrete signals

primitives.name: Spectral Analysis
primitives.formalStatement: Spectral analysis estimates the power spectral density S(f) of a signal, which describes the distribution of power across frequencies. The Wiener-Khinchin theorem relates the PSD to the autocorrelation: S(f) = F{R(tau)} where R(tau) = E[x(t)x(t+tau)].
primitives.computationalForm: Periodogram: S_hat(f) = |X(f)|^2 / N where X(f) is the DFT. Welch's method: divide signal into overlapping segments, window each, compute periodograms, average. Use FFT for efficiency.

dependencies.description: Spectral analysis is based on the Fourier transform
FFT makes spectral estimation computationally feasible

compositionRules.yields: Statistical spectral estimation with confidence intervals
compositionRules.example: Welch's method estimates PSD of EEG signals to identify alpha, beta, and theta brain wave frequencies

applicabilityPatterns: Identifying dominant frequencies in noisy data
Characterizing vibration patterns in mechanical systems
Analyzing periodicity in time series data

primitives.name: Windowing Functions
primitives.formalStatement: A window function w[n] is a finite-length weighting applied to a signal before spectral analysis to reduce spectral leakage. Common windows: rectangular (w[n]=1), Hann (0.5 - 0.5cos(2pi n/N)), Hamming (0.54 - 0.46cos(2pi n/N)), Blackman, Kaiser.
primitives.computationalForm: Apply window by pointwise multiplication: x_w[n] = x[n] * w[n]. Then compute FFT of x_w[n]. The window controls the tradeoff between main lobe width (frequency resolution) and side lobe level (leakage suppression).

dependencies.description: Windows are applied before FFT computation
Window design is analyzed via Fourier transform of the window

compositionRules.yields: Reduced-leakage spectral estimation
compositionRules.example: Applying a Hann window before FFT of a 1024-sample audio clip reduces side lobe artifacts by 31 dB compared to rectangular windowing

applicabilityPatterns: Reducing spectral leakage in finite-length signal analysis
Balancing frequency resolution against side lobe suppression
Preprocessing signals for spectral estimation

primitives.name: Probability Axioms
primitives.formalStatement: Kolmogorov's axioms: For a sample space Omega with sigma-algebra F, a probability measure P satisfies: (1) P(A) >= 0 for all A in F, (2) P(Omega) = 1, (3) P(union A_i) = sum P(A_i) for countably many disjoint events A_i.

compositionRules.yields: Probability space (Omega, F, P) as a measure space
compositionRules.example: Rolling a fair die: Omega = {1,2,3,4,5,6}, F = power set, P({k}) = 1/6 for each k

applicabilityPatterns: Formalizing uncertainty in mathematical models
Defining the foundation for statistical inference
Setting up probability spaces for random experiments

primitives.name: Conditional Probability
primitives.formalStatement: The conditional probability of event A given event B (with P(B) > 0) is P(A|B) = P(A intersect B) / P(B). This defines a new probability measure on the restricted sample space.
primitives.computationalForm: Compute P(A intersect B) and P(B) from the probability model. Divide: P(A|B) = P(A intersect B) / P(B). For independence: P(A|B) = P(A).

dependencies.description: Conditional probability is defined within probability theory

compositionRules.yields: Law of total probability: P(A) = sum_i P(A|B_i)P(B_i)
conditions: Events B_i form a partition of the sample space
compositionRules.example: Probability of rain = P(rain|cloudy)P(cloudy) + P(rain|clear)P(clear)

applicabilityPatterns: Updating beliefs when new evidence is observed
Computing probabilities in sequential experiments
Defining independence and dependence between events

primitives.name: Bayes' Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: P(A|B) = P(B|A) * P(A) / P(B), where P(A) is the prior, P(B|A) is the likelihood, P(B) is the evidence, and P(A|B) is the posterior probability.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Specify prior P(A) (belief before evidence). 2. Compute likelihood P(B|A) (probability of evidence given hypothesis). 3. Compute evidence P(B) = sum_i P(B|A_i)P(A_i) using total probability. 4. Posterior: P(A|B) = P(B|A) * P(A) / P(B).

dependencies.description: Bayes' theorem inverts conditional probability
Founded on probability axioms

compositionRules.yields: Bayesian inference: posterior distribution = likelihood * prior / evidence
compositionRules.example: Updating a Beta(1,1) prior on a coin's bias after observing 7 heads in 10 flips gives Beta(8,4) posterior

applicabilityPatterns: Updating beliefs in light of new evidence
Medical diagnosis: computing disease probability from test results
Combining prior knowledge with observed data for inference

primitives.name: Random Variable
primitives.formalStatement: A random variable X: Omega -> R is a measurable function from the sample space to the real numbers, assigning a numerical value to each outcome. Its distribution is characterized by the CDF F_X(x) = P(X <= x).

dependencies.description: Random variables are functions on probability spaces
Random variables map to real numbers

compositionRules.yields: Joint random variables and multivariate distributions
conditions: Multiple random variables on the same probability space
compositionRules.example: Height X and weight Y of randomly selected people form a bivariate random variable (X,Y) with joint distribution

applicabilityPatterns: Modeling numerical outcomes of random experiments
Defining probability distributions over numerical values
Abstracting uncertain quantities for mathematical analysis

primitives.name: Expected Value
primitives.formalStatement: The expected value of a random variable X is E[X] = sum_x x P(X=x) for discrete X, or E[X] = integral x f_X(x) dx for continuous X with density f_X. Linearity: E[aX + bY] = aE[X] + bE[Y].
primitives.computationalForm: Discrete: sum each value times its probability. Continuous: integrate x * f(x) over the support. For functions: E[g(X)] = sum g(x) P(X=x) or integral g(x) f(x) dx.

dependencies.description: Expected value is a functional of the distribution
Continuous expected value uses integration

compositionRules.yields: Moment generating function M_X(t) = E[e^{tX}]
conditions: E[e^{tX}] exists in a neighborhood of 0
compositionRules.example: For X ~ Poisson(lambda), M_X(t) = exp(lambda(e^t - 1)), from which all moments are derived

applicabilityPatterns: Computing the average outcome of a random process
Evaluating decision alternatives under uncertainty
Defining fairness and equilibrium in probabilistic models

primitives.name: Variance
primitives.formalStatement: The variance of a random variable X is Var(X) = E[(X - E[X])^2] = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2, measuring the expected squared deviation from the mean. Standard deviation sigma = sqrt(Var(X)).
primitives.computationalForm: Compute E[X] and E[X^2]. Then Var(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2. For independent variables: Var(X+Y) = Var(X) + Var(Y). For scaled variables: Var(aX) = a^2 Var(X).

dependencies.description: Variance is defined in terms of expected value
Variance characterizes the distribution of a random variable

compositionRules.yields: Chebyshev's inequality: P(|X - mu| >= k*sigma) <= 1/k^2
compositionRules.example: For any distribution with mean 100 and std 15, at least 75% of values fall within 2 standard deviations: [70, 130]

applicabilityPatterns: Quantifying the spread or dispersion of a distribution
Assessing risk and uncertainty in financial and scientific models
Comparing the reliability of different estimators

primitives.name: Probability Distributions
primitives.formalStatement: A probability distribution assigns probabilities to events. Key distributions: Binomial B(n,p) with PMF C(n,k)p^k(1-p)^{n-k}; Poisson Poi(lambda) with PMF e^{-lambda}lambda^k/k!; Normal N(mu,sigma^2) with PDF (1/(sigma*sqrt(2pi)))exp(-(x-mu)^2/(2sigma^2)).
primitives.computationalForm: Binomial: compute C(n,k) * p^k * (1-p)^(n-k). Poisson: compute exp(-lambda) * lambda^k / k!. Normal: use standard normal tables or erfc function. CDF computed by integration or lookup.

dependencies.description: Distributions describe random variables
Distributions satisfy probability axioms
Continuous distributions use density functions integrated to give probabilities

compositionRules.yields: Conjugate prior-posterior pairs for Bayesian inference
conditions: Prior is conjugate to the likelihood
compositionRules.example: Beta prior with Binomial likelihood gives Beta posterior: Beta(alpha,beta) + Binomial data -> Beta(alpha+successes, beta+failures)

applicabilityPatterns: Modeling specific types of random phenomena with parametric families
Computing probabilities for binomial experiments, rare events, or measurement errors
Selecting appropriate statistical models for data

primitives.name: Normal Distribution
primitives.formalStatement: The normal (Gaussian) distribution N(mu, sigma^2) has PDF f(x) = (1/(sigma*sqrt(2*pi))) * exp(-(x-mu)^2 / (2*sigma^2)). The standard normal Z ~ N(0,1). The 68-95-99.7 rule: 68% within 1 sigma, 95% within 2 sigma, 99.7% within 3 sigma.
primitives.computationalForm: Standardize: Z = (X - mu) / sigma. Use standard normal CDF Phi(z) for probabilities. Inverse: X = mu + sigma * Phi^{-1}(p). Box-Muller transform generates normal samples from uniform samples.

dependencies.description: Normal is a specific probability distribution family
Normal distribution is parametrized by mean and variance

compositionRules.yields: Maximum likelihood estimation for normal parameters
compositionRules.example: MLE for N(mu, sigma^2): mu_hat = sample mean, sigma^2_hat = sample variance

applicabilityPatterns: Modeling measurement errors and natural variation
Serving as the limiting distribution in the central limit theorem
Providing the foundation for many statistical tests

primitives.name: Markov Chain
primitives.formalStatement: A Markov chain is a stochastic process {X_n} where the future state depends only on the present state, not the past: P(X_{n+1} = j | X_n = i, X_{n-1}, ..., X_0) = P(X_{n+1} = j | X_n = i) = p_{ij}. The transition matrix P = [p_{ij}] is row-stochastic.
primitives.computationalForm: Given initial distribution pi_0 and transition matrix P, the distribution at step n is pi_n = pi_0 * P^n. Stationary distribution pi satisfies pi * P = pi. Compute P^n by matrix exponentiation or eigendecomposition.

dependencies.description: Markov property is a conditional independence statement
Transition probabilities form a probability distribution
Transition matrix is a linear operator on probability vectors

compositionRules.yields: Stationary distribution computation via eigenvector of transition matrix
compositionRules.example: PageRank: the stationary distribution of a web-graph Markov chain gives the importance of each page

applicabilityPatterns: Modeling systems that evolve randomly with memoryless transitions
Analyzing long-run behavior of stochastic processes
Computing page rank and random walk probabilities on graphs

primitives.name: Law of Large Numbers
primitives.formalStatement: Weak LLN: For iid random variables X_1, ..., X_n with mean mu, the sample mean X_bar_n = (1/n) sum X_i converges in probability to mu: P(|X_bar_n - mu| > epsilon) -> 0 as n -> infinity. Strong LLN: convergence is almost sure.
primitives.computationalForm: For a sample of size n from a distribution with mean mu, the sample mean converges to mu. Error bounds: by Chebyshev, P(|X_bar - mu| > epsilon) <= sigma^2/(n*epsilon^2). For practical purposes, n > 30 gives reasonable convergence for most distributions.

dependencies.description: LLN concerns convergence to the expected value
Finite variance ensures convergence rate
Convergence is defined in terms of probability

compositionRules.yields: Monte Carlo estimation: approximate E[g(X)] by sample average
compositionRules.example: Estimate pi by sampling random points in [0,1]^2: pi/4 ≈ fraction falling inside the unit circle, converging by LLN

applicabilityPatterns: Justifying the use of sample averages to estimate population parameters
Understanding why larger samples give more reliable estimates
Providing the theoretical basis for Monte Carlo simulation

primitives.name: Central Limit Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: For iid random variables X_1, ..., X_n with mean mu and variance sigma^2, the standardized sum (X_bar_n - mu) / (sigma/sqrt(n)) converges in distribution to N(0,1) as n -> infinity.
primitives.computationalForm: For large n, approximate P(X_bar_n <= x) ≈ Phi((x - mu) / (sigma/sqrt(n))) where Phi is the standard normal CDF. Rule of thumb: n >= 30 is sufficient for most distributions; for highly skewed distributions, larger n may be needed.

dependencies.description: CLT shows convergence to the normal distribution
Finite variance is required for the classical CLT
CLT refines LLN by characterizing the distribution of deviations

compositionRules.yields: Confidence intervals and hypothesis tests for sample means
compositionRules.example: For n=100 samples with mean 50 and std 10, a 95% CI for the population mean is 50 +/- 1.96*10/sqrt(100) = [48.04, 51.96]

applicabilityPatterns: Explaining why the normal distribution appears so frequently in nature
Constructing confidence intervals and hypothesis tests for large samples
Justifying normal approximations to sums of random variables

primitives.name: Transfer Function
primitives.formalStatement: The transfer function H(s) of a continuous-time LTI system is the Laplace transform of the impulse response h(t): H(s) = L{h(t)} = integral_0^inf h(t) e^{-st} dt. For discrete-time: H(z) = Z{h[n]}. Input-output: Y(s) = H(s) X(s).
primitives.computationalForm: For a system described by differential equation sum a_k y^(k) = sum b_k x^(k), H(s) = B(s)/A(s) where B(s) = sum b_k s^k, A(s) = sum a_k s^k. Evaluate frequency response: H(j*omega) = H(s)|_{s=j*omega}.

dependencies.description: Transfer function evaluated on the imaginary axis gives the frequency response
Transfer functions encode differential equation systems
Transfer function represents a linear operator

compositionRules.yields: Complete system design from specification to implementation
compositionRules.example: Design a Butterworth low-pass filter: specify cutoff frequency and order, compute H(s) = 1/B_n(s/omega_c), realize as circuit or digital filter

applicabilityPatterns: Analyzing the frequency response of linear systems
Designing control systems with specified stability and performance
Converting between time-domain and frequency-domain system descriptions

primitives.name: Data Compression
primitives.formalStatement: Data compression reduces the number of bits needed to represent information. Lossless compression (Huffman, LZW, arithmetic coding) preserves all information with compression ratio bounded by entropy. Lossy compression (JPEG, MP3) discards information below perceptual thresholds.
primitives.computationalForm: Lossless: compute symbol frequencies, build Huffman tree or use arithmetic coder. Lossy: transform (DCT/FFT), quantize coefficients, entropy code the quantized values. Compression ratio = original size / compressed size.

dependencies.description: Entropy sets the fundamental limit for lossless compression
Huffman coding is a key lossless compression technique
Transform coding uses frequency-domain representations for lossy compression

compositionRules.yields: Transform-based lossy compression (JPEG, MP3)
compositionRules.example: JPEG: DCT of 8x8 blocks, quantize high-frequency coefficients aggressively, Huffman code the result

applicabilityPatterns: Reducing storage and bandwidth requirements for data
Exploiting statistical redundancy in information sources
Trading off fidelity for compression in multimedia applications

primitives.name: Noisy Channel Coding Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: For any rate R < C (channel capacity), there exist codes of rate R that achieve arbitrarily small error probability. Conversely, for R > C, the error probability is bounded away from zero for all codes. Proved by Shannon using random coding arguments.
primitives.computationalForm: For a binary symmetric channel with crossover probability p: C = 1 - H(p). Design codes with rate R < C and block length n. Error probability decreases exponentially: P_e <= 2^{-nE(R)} where E(R) is the reliability function.

dependencies.description: The theorem characterizes achievability relative to capacity
Entropy enters through the capacity formula
Random coding argument uses probabilistic existence proof

compositionRules.yields: Practical capacity-approaching coding schemes
compositionRules.example: Turbo codes and LDPC codes achieve within 0.1 dB of Shannon capacity on AWGN channels with practical decoding algorithms

applicabilityPatterns: Understanding the fundamental limits of reliable communication
Motivating the design of efficient error-correcting codes
Proving that reliable communication is possible over noisy channels

primitives.name: Entropy Rate
primitives.formalStatement: The entropy rate of a stochastic process {X_n} is H' = lim_{n->inf} (1/n) H(X_1, X_2, ..., X_n), measuring the average information per symbol in the long run.
primitives.computationalForm: For a stationary Markov chain with transition matrix P and stationary distribution pi: H' = -sum_i pi_i sum_j P_{ij} log P_{ij}.

dependencies.description: Entropy rate extends Shannon entropy to sequential processes
Markov chains provide the simplest nontrivial entropy rate computation

compositionRules.yields: Optimal compression ratio for sequential data equals the entropy rate
compositionRules.example: English text has entropy rate ~1.0-1.5 bits/character, so optimal compression approaches this limit

applicabilityPatterns: Measuring information content of sequential data
Determining optimal compression rates for time series

primitives.name: Rate-Distortion Theory
primitives.formalStatement: The rate-distortion function R(D) gives the minimum bit rate required to represent a source with average distortion at most D. R(D) = min_{p(x_hat|x): E[d(X,X_hat)]<=D} I(X; X_hat).
primitives.computationalForm: For a Gaussian source X ~ N(0, sigma^2) with squared-error distortion: R(D) = 0.5 * log_2(sigma^2/D) for 0 < D <= sigma^2, and R(D) = 0 for D > sigma^2.

dependencies.description: Rate-distortion function is defined as a minimum mutual information
Entropy provides the upper bound at zero distortion

compositionRules.yields: Spectral rate-distortion: frequency-domain allocation of bits to spectral components
conditions: Source is stationary with known power spectral density
compositionRules.example: Reverse water-filling allocates more bits to high-energy frequency bands for optimal lossy compression

applicabilityPatterns: Determining theoretical limits of lossy compression
Optimizing quality vs bitrate tradeoffs in signal encoding

primitives.name: Discrete Cosine Transform
primitives.formalStatement: The DCT-II of a sequence x[n] of length N is X[k] = sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] cos[pi/N * (n+0.5) * k] for k = 0, 1, ..., N-1. It concentrates energy in low-frequency coefficients for natural signals.
primitives.computationalForm: Compute via FFT of length 2N on symmetrically extended input: O(N log N). The inverse DCT recovers x[n] from X[k] with normalization. Used in JPEG (8x8 blocks) and MP3 (MDCT variant).

dependencies.description: DCT is a real-valued specialization of the Fourier transform
DCT can be computed efficiently via FFT

compositionRules.yields: Transform-based lossy compression: quantize DCT coefficients for image/audio compression
conditions: Input signal has energy concentrated in low frequencies
compositionRules.example: JPEG compression applies 8x8 DCT, quantizes coefficients, then entropy codes the result

applicabilityPatterns: Efficient real-valued frequency decomposition of signals
Energy compaction for transform-based compression

primitives.name: Moment Generating Function
primitives.formalStatement: The moment generating function of a random variable X is M_X(t) = E[e^{tX}] = integral e^{tx} f(x) dx. The nth moment is E[X^n] = M_X^{(n)}(0) (nth derivative evaluated at t=0).
primitives.computationalForm: For discrete X: M_X(t) = sum_x e^{tx} P(X=x). For X ~ N(mu, sigma^2): M_X(t) = exp(mu*t + sigma^2*t^2/2). Uniquely determines distribution when it exists in a neighborhood of 0.

dependencies.description: MGF is defined as the expected value of e^{tX}
Each distribution has a characteristic MGF

compositionRules.yields: Proof of CLT via convergence of MGFs to the Gaussian MGF
conditions: MGF exists in a neighborhood of 0
compositionRules.example: Sum of n i.i.d. variables: M_{S_n/sqrt(n)}(t) -> exp(t^2/2) as n -> infinity, proving convergence to standard normal

applicabilityPatterns: Computing moments of probability distributions systematically
Proving convergence in distribution via MGF convergence

primitives.name: Joint Probability Distribution
primitives.formalStatement: The joint distribution of random variables X, Y is P(X in A, Y in B) for all measurable sets A, B. For continuous variables, the joint density f_{X,Y}(x,y) satisfies P(X in A, Y in B) = integral_A integral_B f(x,y) dy dx.
primitives.computationalForm: Marginal: f_X(x) = integral f_{X,Y}(x,y) dy. Independence: f_{X,Y}(x,y) = f_X(x)*f_Y(y). Covariance: Cov(X,Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y]. Correlation: rho = Cov(X,Y)/(sigma_X * sigma_Y).

dependencies.description: Joint distributions are defined over multiple random variables
Joint distributions satisfy the axioms of probability

compositionRules.yields: Posterior inference: compute conditional distributions from joint via Bayes rule
conditions: Joint distribution is known or estimable
Marginal of conditioning variable is nonzero
compositionRules.example: Given joint P(X,Y), compute P(Y|X=x) = P(X=x,Y)/P(X=x) for Bayesian parameter estimation

applicabilityPatterns: Modeling relationships between multiple random variables
Computing conditional distributions and independence properties
